column7: Description

The 11th Woman's Murder Club case finds Detective Lindsay Boxer racing to stop a serial killer targeting young women in San Francisco. James Patterson delivers his trademark short chapters and relentless pacing that make it impossible to put down. A gripping instalment in the beloved Women's Murder Club series.

In the ninth Women's Murder Club thriller, a shocking courtroom killing sends Lindsay Boxer and her team into a web of high-profile murder and legal intrigue. Patterson keeps the tension wire-tight across every chapter. Fans of the series will find this one of its most satisfying entries.

Set against the rugged beauty of a Cornish fishing village, this warm-hearted novel follows two families whose lives become intertwined across generations. Fern Britton writes with compassion and a deep understanding of community, loyalty, and the secrets families keep. A deeply satisfying read for fans of women's fiction.

A country house murder mystery with a sharp wit and a cast of delightfully eccentric suspects. Alex Coombs serves up a classic whodunit with modern sensibilities, anchored by an engaging amateur sleuth. Perfect for fans of traditional British crime fiction.

FBI Agent Atlee Pine reopens the cold case of her twin sister's abduction while pursuing a serial killer in her Georgia hometown. Baldacci expertly weaves the personal and the procedural into a nail-biting thriller. The second Atlee Pine novel confirms her as one of crime fiction's most compelling heroines.

Set in Cornwall at the turn of the twentieth century, this saga follows a young woman navigating poverty, heartbreak, and the cruel realities of the era. Rosie Goodwin brings her trademark emotional depth to this moving family story. Rich in period detail and populated with unforgettable characters.

A warm and witty romantic novel set in the English countryside, following a woman who unexpectedly inherits a crumbling estate and the life that comes with it. Katie Fforde is at her sparkling best, blending charm, humour, and a deeply satisfying romance. Ideal for fans of feel-good fiction.

column2: A Short Story Of Nearly Everything
column7: Bill Bryson turns his restless curiosity onto the history of nearly everything, from the Big Bang to the rise of civilisation. Written with his signature humour and infectious wonder, this is popular science at its most accessible and entertaining. One of the most beloved science books of the past two decades.

Set in post-independence India, this sweeping epic follows young Lata and her mother's quest to find her a suitable husband across four intertwined families. Vikram Seth's 1,400-page masterpiece is both an intimate love story and a vast panoramic portrait of a nation at a crossroads. One of the great novels of the twentieth century.

A mysterious woman appears at Nick's car window in the middle of the night with a chilling warning — and then vanishes. Linwood Barclay ratchets up the suburban paranoia with his customary skill, delivering a thriller that keeps you second-guessing until the final page. Compulsive reading.

A warmly observed tale of village life in Yorkshire, following a cast of brilliantly realised characters through love, loss, and the everyday dramas of a tight-knit community. Julie Houston writes with infectious humour and genuine heart. Fans of Joanna Trollope and Jill Mansell will feel right at home.

Bill Bryson walks the 2,000-mile Appalachian Trail — with hilariously mixed results. Part adventure, part natural history, part comedy, this memoir is a joyful celebration of the American wilderness and the absurdity of middle-aged ambition. One of the funniest travel books ever written.

A body found buried under a frozen Scottish loch leads detective Fin Macleod back to his Hebridean roots and a community guarding old secrets. Peter May's evocative prose makes the Scottish landscape as much a character as the people who inhabit it. A brooding, atmospheric thriller.

column2: A Year At The Star And Sixpence
column7: Holly Hepburn's second Star and Sixpence novel follows the Rhys sisters as their Cornish village pub faces new challenges and old secrets resurface. Warm, funny, and full of heart, this is comfort reading of the finest order. Perfect for a cosy afternoon.

A woman survives a devastating diagnosis and must decide what kind of life she wants to build in the time she has. Clare Mackintosh writes with emotional precision about grief, identity, and the courage it takes to choose happiness. Genuinely moving and beautifully constructed.

Based on the true story of a nineteenth-century Canadian woman convicted of murdering her employer, Margaret Atwood reconstructs Grace Marks' life with extraordinary psychological depth. Alias Grace is a masterwork of historical fiction — unsettling, compassionate, and utterly gripping. Winner of the Giller Prize.

A tender romantic novel following a young woman whose life takes an unexpected turn when she meets two very different men on the same day. Melissa Hill writes with warmth and a sharp eye for the complications of modern love. Ideal for fans of Marian Keyes.

James Herriot's classic memoir of life as a young vet in the Yorkshire Dales is one of the warmest, funniest books ever written. Filled with unforgettable animals and even more unforgettable farmers, it is a love letter to the English countryside. Beloved by generations of readers worldwide.

A deeply felt novel about memory, identity, and the unexpected friendships that sustain us through loss. Rosie Thomas writes with quiet elegance about middle age, reinvention, and the parts of ourselves we carry forward. A thoughtful and emotionally rewarding read.

Detective Tess DeMarco is haunted by a case gone wrong — and hunted by the man she helped put away. Karen Rose delivers relentless suspense with emotionally complex characters who make you care deeply about what happens next. A standout entry in the Cincinnati series.

Frank McCourt's Pulitzer Prize-winning memoir of growing up in desperate poverty in Limerick, Ireland is told with devastating honesty and extraordinary dark humour. Angela's Ashes is a book that breaks your heart and then somehow lifts it. One of the greatest memoirs ever written.

Set in Birmingham during the Second World War, this moving saga follows a woman who discovers her husband has fathered a child with another woman and must decide whether to raise the boy as her own. Anne Bennett writes with compassion and grit about the resilience of women in wartime. A deeply affecting read.

When the Delaney family patriarch disappears on the anniversary of a mysterious event, old wounds and buried secrets begin to resurface. Liane Moriarty brings her trademark wit and psychological precision to this page-turning domestic thriller. Compulsive, clever, and emotionally rich.

Bill Bryson turns his curiosity onto the history of domestic life — the remarkable stories behind every room, object, and habit in the modern home. Ranging across centuries and continents, At Home is endlessly fascinating popular history. Bryson at his most delightfully informative.

Ben Goldacre's brilliant, witty demolition of pseudoscience, bad statistics, and the misleading health claims that fill newspapers and supplement bottles. Bad Science is essential reading for anyone who has ever been baffled by a health scare headline. Eye-opening and genuinely important.

A glamorous producer discovers a hidden gem of a theatre in a small English town and finds her life transformed in unexpected ways. Danielle Steel writes with her characteristic warmth about reinvention, second chances, and the courage to follow your dreams. An uplifting and escapist read.

Dawn French's debut novel is a big-hearted, funny, and surprisingly moving story about love, motherhood, and the lengths we go to for our children. Written with French's natural comic timing and deep warmth, it is a genuine delight from start to finish.

Belle is the extraordinary story of a mixed-race girl raised in eighteenth-century England, navigating aristocratic society with grace and determination. Lesley Pearse brings emotional depth and vivid period detail to this richly imagined historical novel. A powerful story of identity, dignity, and love.

A seemingly routine translation job becomes a deadly game of espionage when Harry Barnett discovers a hidden manuscript that someone will kill to keep secret. Robert Goddard's trademark blend of literary intelligence and breathless plotting is at its finest here. A superior British thriller.

When a bone marrow donor is murdered, Detective Harry Bosch discovers the dead man may have been the only person who could save a dying boy's life. Michael Connelly weaves moral complexity into a tightly plotted mystery that shows why Bosch is one of crime fiction's greatest detectives. Exceptional.

The fourth and final book in Stephenie Meyer's Twilight saga brings Bella and Edward's story to its epic conclusion with a wedding, a honeymoon, and a transformation that changes everything. Meyer delivers the emotional payoff her millions of fans had been waiting for. Sweeping and emotionally satisfying.

When a young woman is found savagely murdered in Atlanta, Detectives Will Trent and Sara Linton are pulled into a case that cuts dangerously close to home. Karin Slaughter writes crime fiction with unflinching honesty about violence against women. Dark, brilliant, and impossible to put down.

Joanna Trollope's quietly devastating novel follows the fallout when an adopted brother and sister discover details about their birth parents. Trollope dissects family dynamics with her customary precision and empathy. A thoughtful, beautifully observed study of identity and belonging.

A collection of illustrated postcards arrives at a Greek island, each one telling a piece of a stranger's story. Victoria Hislop weaves a rich, sun-drenched narrative about love, loss, and the transformative power of travel. Romantic, evocative, and deeply transportive.

A cosy Christmas romance set in the snowy mountains, following two strangers forced together by circumstance who discover something unexpected in each other. Nora Roberts delivers exactly the warmth, charm, and romantic satisfaction you want from a festive read.

A cyclist is found dead on a remote Hebridean island, and the investigation draws Fin Macleod into a labyrinthine conspiracy with roots in the past. Peter May's second Hebrides thriller is as atmospheric and compelling as its predecessor. The island landscape is rendered with stunning precision.

When Ellie arrives in a small Scottish village to manage a new project, she finds a community of wonderfully eccentric characters — and a man she wasn't expecting. Rachael Lucas writes with warmth and wry humour about belonging, second chances, and the courage to start over. Irresistibly charming.

Set during the plague years of the fourteenth century, nine travellers shelter together and pass the time by telling stories — but one of them is not who they claim to be. Karen Maitland's atmospheric historical thriller is genuinely unsettling and brilliantly researched. A dark masterpiece.

A self-confessed sociopath provides a chilling insider account of how she manipulates, deceives, and navigates a world built for empaths. M.E. Thomas writes with disconcerting candour about a psychology most of us will never understand. Disturbing, fascinating, and impossible to dismiss.

A comprehensive and practical reference guide to English grammar, usage, and style, covering everything from punctuation rules to common errors. Clear, accessible, and authoritative — an essential desk companion for writers, students, and professionals.

Set against the backdrop of World War Two's North African campaign, Wilbur Smith follows the Courtney family through some of the conflict's most brutal and pivotal battles. Smith writes military adventure with tremendous scope and visceral energy. A gripping page-turner for fans of historical action.

Elizabeth Gaskell's gentle, witty portrait of life in the small English town of Cranford is a masterclass in quiet observation and social comedy. Though the characters face change and loss, the tone is warm and the humanity boundless. A Victorian classic that still feels utterly fresh.

When a man receives a terrifying ransom note claiming his daughter has been taken, the nightmare is compounded by one brutal fact — his daughter is safe at home. Harlan Coben's hero Myron Bolitar is at his most resourceful in this labyrinthine thriller. Impossible to put down.

A rich and fascinating history of the women who accompanied British diplomats abroad in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, navigating foreign courts with astonishing adaptability. Katie Hickman writes with elegance and a genuine gift for bringing historical women to life. Absorbing and enlightening.

The sweeping first novel in Santa Montefiore's Deverill Chronicles follows the daughters of an Anglo-Irish family across decades of war, love, and loss. Montefiore writes lavish, emotionally immersive historical fiction. Perfect for fans of Downton Abbey-style family sagas.

Jade must uncover the truth about her missing mother while protecting herself from dangerous enemies who want to keep the past buried. Lesley Pearse delivers a gripping thriller with her trademark emotional depth and a heroine you will root for completely. Compelling and satisfying.

Forensic anthropologist Temperance Brennan is called in when a series of ancient bones turns out to be not so ancient — and the killer is still active. Kathy Reichs launched one of crime fiction's most iconic characters with this debut. The basis for the long-running TV series Bones.

Wilbur Smith's epic story of the North African desert war follows Sean Courtney through the brutal campaign against Rommel's Afrika Korps. Vivid, visceral, and magnificently researched, this is historical adventure writing at its sweeping best. A must for fans of Smith's Courtney saga.

Set in Edwardian Lancashire, Anna Jacobs tells the story of a young woman determined to build a better life through her own ingenuity and fierce determination. A richly detailed portrait of working-class life and the power of ambition. Warm-hearted and completely engrossing.

A comprehensive reference compiling thousands of common English phrases, idioms, and their origins, arranged for easy browsing. An invaluable resource for writers, language enthusiasts, and anyone curious about where everyday expressions come from.

When the body of a pupil is discovered at St Oswald's Grammar School, history teacher Guy Fielding finds himself entangled in a web of dark secrets stretching back decades. Joanne Harris writes psychological suspense with the same lush intensity she brings to her literary novels. Brilliantly unsettling.

Anthony Trollope's Barsetshire novel follows the idealistic Dr Thorne as he navigates questions of class, inheritance, and the rigid social hierarchies of Victorian England. A richly characterised and deeply humane novel from one of the great Victorian masters. Wonderfully readable.

A high-stakes thriller in which investigative reporter Ben Hawkins stumbles onto a conspiracy that puts him directly in a killer's crosshairs. Patterson delivers his signature breakneck pacing and short, punchy chapters that make this compulsively unputdownable. A great entry point to Patterson's standalone thrillers.

Barack Obama's memoir of his early life traces his search for identity through his Kenyan father's story, his Kansas-born mother's influence, and his years as a community organiser in Chicago. Beautifully written and deeply introspective, it reveals the making of one of the most remarkable political figures of our time.

Joshua Levine's gripping account of the Dunkirk evacuation draws on diaries, letters, and testimonies to tell the story from every angle — the soldiers on the beach, the civilians in the little boats, and the commanders making desperate decisions. An essential piece of Second World War history.

Bryony Gordon's searingly honest memoir chronicles her struggle with mental illness, addiction, and the unexpected salvation she found in running. Raw, funny, and extraordinarily brave, this is a book about how we survive the battles no one else can see. Genuinely life-affirming.

The third book in the Twilight saga deepens the love triangle between Bella, Edward, and Jacob as a dangerous new threat forces old enemies into an uneasy alliance. Stephenie Meyer balances romantic tension and supernatural action with real skill. A satisfying middle chapter in the saga.

Lincoln Rhyme and Amelia Sachs face a killer who can predict their every move in this taut, cerebral thriller from Jeffrey Deaver. The plotting is ferociously intricate and the final twist genuinely surprises. Deaver at his most ingenious.

In the second Inheritance Cycle novel, Eragon and his dragon Saphira continue their perilous journey, discovering new allies and ancient secrets that deepen the scope of Paolini's world. A massive, richly imagined fantasy epic that builds impressively on its predecessor.

Stephen Baxter's gripping historical novel reimagines the last days of Roman Britain through the story of a soldier navigating an empire in collapse. Meticulous research meets compelling storytelling. A must for readers who love their history laced with drama.

Fifteen-year-old Christopher Paolini's debut novel launched one of fantasy's most beloved sagas — the story of a farm boy who discovers a dragon egg and is drawn into an ancient conflict. Eragon is a stunning achievement for any writer, let alone a teenager. Epic, imaginative, and wholly absorbing.

A tightly wound thriller in which a man on the run from a deadly conspiracy must protect a woman who holds the key to everything. Andrew Gross delivers the high-octane plotting of his former collaborator James Patterson with a satisfying emotional core. Gripping from first page to last.

Harry Bosch investigates a cold case murder while rookie reporter Madison Purl races to break the story — only to find herself in the killer's sights. Connelly orchestrates dual timelines with his customary mastery, delivering a thriller that is both technically brilliant and emotionally resonant.

When Nick Garber's son disappears after sending a cryptic text, a seemingly ordinary suburb reveals layers of deception, violence, and buried secrets. Linwood Barclay is at his suburban thriller best — quietly chilling, relentlessly plotted, impossible to put down.

Another compulsive entry in Linwood Barclay's catalogue of suburban paranoia, in which a seemingly simple lie cascades into something deadly and irreversible. Barclay writes the slow burn of domestic dread better than almost anyone. A thoroughly satisfying thriller.

When Harry Barnett is hired to investigate a fifty-year-old mystery in Vienna, he uncovers a conspiracy that stretches from the Cold War to the present day. Robert Goddard's meticulous plotting and gift for historical atmosphere make this one of his finest novels. A thinking person's thriller.

The second book in E.L. James's blockbuster series plunges Ana and Christian deeper into their complicated relationship as Ana begins to understand the darkness beneath Christian's control. James delivers the emotional intensity and romantic drama that made the series a global phenomenon.

The third and final instalment in the Fifty Shades trilogy brings Ana and Christian's tumultuous journey to its emotional conclusion. E.L. James delivers the resolution her millions of fans were waiting for with characteristic passion and drama.

E.L. James's debut novel redefined popular romance fiction, introducing the world to the magnetic and troubled Christian Grey and the innocent Anastasia Steele. Whatever one thinks of its literary merits, it sparked a global conversation about desire, power, and women's fiction. A cultural phenomenon.

A comprehensive guide to using everyday foods as natural medicine, exploring the science behind how specific ingredients can prevent and treat common health conditions. Jean Carper draws on clinical research to make a compelling case for food as our most powerful health tool.

SAS trooper Nick Stone goes deep undercover in a mission that takes him from the streets of Colombia to the corridors of Washington power. Andy McNab's military thriller writing is visceral, authentic, and propulsive. For Valour is McNab at his most assured.

A man inherits a Devon estate and with it a mystery stretching back to the First World War — and someone very much alive wants the truth to stay buried. Robert Goddard's intricate construction and patient storytelling reward every reader who trusts his lead. A masterful slow-burn thriller.

Set in a small Cornish village in the aftermath of the Second World War, this deeply felt saga follows a young woman rebuilding her life among the scars her community carries. Gloria Cook writes with warmth and sensitivity about resilience, community, and the long shadow of war.

When shy teenager Penny starts a blog to document her life, her world changes overnight and she must navigate overnight fame, first love, and the blurred line between public and private. Zoe Sugg's debut novel captures the anxieties of growing up in the digital age with genuine insight.

Penny and Noah take their relationship on the road as her blog goes global and the pressure of fame threatens everything she loves. Zoe Sugg delivers a charming sequel with heart, humour, and a message about staying true to yourself. Perfect for young adult readers and beyond.

Vex King's combination of self-help principles and spiritual philosophy has resonated with millions of readers looking for practical guidance on positivity, purpose, and building a life that feels meaningful. Accessible, warm, and genuinely motivating.

A Cotswolds setting, an amateur sleuth with a complicated past, and a murder that reveals the village's darkest secrets — Rebecca Tope's gentle mystery delivers everything cosy crime fans love. Atmospheric, elegantly plotted, and deeply satisfying.

A Yale law student abandons a lucrative corporate career to intern at a tiny legal aid clinic in the Appalachian coalfields — and discovers just how corrupt the system she wanted to join really is. Grisham at his most politically engaged, writing with genuine anger and compassion.

Jonathan Swift's satirical masterpiece follows Lemuel Gulliver through four extraordinary voyages to fantastical lands, each one a devastating critique of human nature, politics, and society. One of the most brilliantly subversive works in the English language. Still essential, still shockingly relevant.

Detective Alex Cross is drawn into a deadly game of hide-and-seek with a cunning killer who seems always one step ahead. Patterson's signature short chapters and relentless plotting make this one of the most compulsively readable entries in the Cross series.

A woman in witness protection begins to believe that someone in her new life knows exactly who she is. Susan Lewis builds psychological suspense with real skill, anchoring the thriller mechanics in deeply human emotional territory. Gripping and surprisingly moving.

A deeply strange and brilliantly written novel following a terminally ill alcoholic's journey through London's underworld of addiction, hallucination, and dark comedy. Will Self at his most challenging and rewarding — this is literary fiction that refuses to make things easy.

A clear and accessible guide to mathematical concepts for secondary school students, covering key topics with worked examples and practice problems. An invaluable resource for students preparing for exams.

Wilbur Smith's ocean adventure follows Nicholas Berg, salvage expert and reluctant hero, on a race against time and tide to rescue a stricken supertanker. Ferociously paced and magnificently researched, this is Smith at his thrilling maritime best.

The extraordinary true story of a Jewish child advertised in a 1938 newspaper by a desperate father seeking a kind family to take in his son — and the decades-long journey to uncover what became of him. Julian Borger writes with extraordinary compassion and moral precision.

When a brutal murder in a peaceful German town reveals a hidden network of secrets, detectives Pia Kirchhoff and Oliver von Bodenstein must untangle a conspiracy that reaches into the highest levels of society. Nele Neuhaus writes chillingly effective crime fiction.

Claire Tomalin's acclaimed biography of Jane Austen pieces together the life of the most beloved novelist in the English language from fragmentary evidence, family letters, and deep literary intelligence. Essential reading for anyone who loves Austen's work.

H. Rider Haggard's 1885 adventure classic follows Allan Quatermain into the heart of Africa in search of a lost king and legendary mines. A founding text of the adventure genre, it still reads with tremendous propulsive energy and colourful invention.

Brian Tracy distils decades of business experience into the core laws that govern sustained professional success — from clarity of purpose to the discipline of execution. Practical, direct, and packed with actionable insights. Essential reading for anyone building a career or business.

After a shipwreck strands Pi Patel on a lifeboat with a Bengal tiger, the young boy must find a way to survive — and to understand the nature of story itself. Yann Martel's Booker Prize-winning novel is a dazzling meditation on faith, survival, and the stories we need to live. Extraordinary.

A second copy of Yann Martel's extraordinary Booker Prize winner — the story of Pi Patel's 227 days adrift on the Pacific Ocean with a 450-pound Bengal tiger. Beautiful, strange, and profoundly moving. One of the most original novels of the twenty-first century.

Charles Dickens's sprawling masterpiece of Victorian London, reimagined for television by Andrew Davies, follows the innocent Amy Dorrit as she navigates poverty, prison, and the corrupt world of money and ambition. A richly detailed adaptation of one of Dickens's most humane novels.

Myron Bolitar is called back into action when his old flame's husband is found dead — and she's the prime suspect. Harlan Coben delivers his trademark blend of wit, warmth, and heart-stopping suspense. The series continues to be one of the most reliably entertaining in crime fiction.

When a wolf biologist is shot and his pack killed, his family is torn apart by grief and blame in ways that reveal deep fractures in all of their relationships. Jodi Picoult examines the collision between human and animal worlds with her customary emotional intelligence. Thought-provoking and beautifully researched.

Decades after a train accident, a retired detective receives a letter that reopens the case — and sets him on a trail that will uncover a conspiracy of breathtaking scope. Robert Goddard at his most patient and most brilliant, delivering a finale that pays off every page of investment.

When a woman's body is found in the Los Angeles River, Harry Bosch discovers she was a witness to an unsolved murder and someone is determined to keep the past buried. Connelly's plotting is immaculate and his portrait of a city's moral complexity unparalleled. A series highlight.

A warm and witty romantic comedy following a cycling journalist whose life is turned upside down by an unexpected encounter and a series of very bad decisions. Freya North writes with sparkling humour and genuine emotional intelligence. Perfect for fans of joyful, character-driven romance.

column2: Midnight in the garden of Good and Evil
column7: John Berendt's non-fiction account of the high society murder trial in Savannah, Georgia is one of the most compulsively readable true crime books ever written. Part travelogue, part character study, and part courtroom drama, it captures a world unlike any other. A modern classic.

Set in an unnamed city in the Troubles-era Northern Ireland, Anna Burns's Booker Prize-winning novel follows an eighteen-year-old girl being stalked by a powerful paramilitary figure. Written in a singular, breathless style that demands and rewards complete attention. Profoundly brilliant.

In 1950, two very different women set off on an expedition to the jungles of New Caledonia in search of a rare beetle, and the consequences will define their lives. Rachel Joyce writes with wit, warmth, and a deep understanding of female friendship. A complete joy from first page to last.

Set in a northern English industrial town in the early twentieth century, this saga follows the indomitable Martha Mary Crawford as she sacrifices her own happiness to hold her family together. Catherine Cookson at her most powerful, writing about female strength with trademark emotional force.

Wilbur Smith's epic novel spans two continents and several generations of the Courtney family, from the Anglo-Boer War to the birth of Zimbabwe. A sweeping adventure of love, war, and betrayal told with Smith's characteristic energy and moral complexity. One of his finest achievements.

A hilarious compendium of the habits, obsessions, and social rituals that define the peculiarly British experience, from queuing etiquette to passive-aggressive apologies. Rob Temple's wit is perfectly calibrated. Ideal for anyone who has ever felt that being British is simultaneously wonderful and completely baffling.

The companion volume to Rob Temple's first celebration of British awkwardness, this sequel digs deeper into the psychology of a nation that would rather suffer in silence than cause a fuss. Brilliantly observed and endlessly quotable.

Ken Follett's massive standalone novel follows a group of strangers connected by a crisis that unfolds across multiple continents over a single week. Never is Follett at his most ambitious — a geopolitical thriller of extraordinary scope and technical brilliance.

The second Twilight novel plunges Bella into her darkest period yet when Edward leaves Forks and she turns to Jacob Black for comfort. Stephenie Meyer captures teenage heartbreak with raw honesty, and the Jacob-Bella dynamic adds new emotional complexity to the saga.

Jack Reacher walks into a small-town diner and immediately senses he's stumbled into something dangerous. Lee Child's eighteenth Reacher novel is one of the series' most propulsive — a masterclass in thriller mechanics and the quiet menace of a man you do not want to cross.

Harry Bosch investigates a series of murders connected to Hong Kong's triads in one of the most geographically ambitious entries in the series. Connelly expands Bosch's world brilliantly, delivering a thriller that is both culturally rich and compulsively plotted.

When a family is held hostage in a suburban home invasion, the tendrils of the conspiracy they've stumbled into reach far beyond anything they could have imagined. Linwood Barclay at his most ingeniously plotted — suburban terror at its finest.

A young girl abandoned by her mother must navigate poverty, cruelty, and the relentless stigma of illegitimacy in post-war London. Kitty Neale writes with compassion and unflinching honesty about the lives of working-class women. Emotional, gripping, and deeply human.

Dr Ruth Galloway investigates a series of murders connected to a historic theatre, uncovering secrets buried beneath the foundations of a very English institution. Elly Griffiths writes her forensic archaeologist series with tremendous wit and genuine atmosphere.

Set on the Greek island of Spetses in the aftermath of the Second World War, this richly atmospheric novel tells the story of forbidden love, long-kept secrets, and the night that changed everything. Victoria Hislop at her most evocative.

A heartbreaking and life-affirming novel about a young woman with a mysterious gift who changes the lives of everyone she meets before her past catches up with her. Rachel Joyce writes with the same quiet, devastating power that made The Unlikely Pilgrimage of Harold Fry a classic.

When an Amish teenager is found guilty of infanticide, investigative reporter Katie Fisher travels to Pennsylvania Dutch Country to uncover the truth. Jodi Picoult blends legal thriller with deeply compassionate cultural observation. A typically powerful and thought-provoking Picoult.

A murdered woman is the centrepiece of a bizarre public art installation — and Myron Bolitar is hired by the victim's devastated family to find out why. Harlan Coben delivers his trademark blend of suspense, dark humour, and surprisingly heartfelt emotion. Compulsive.

Set in sixteenth-century Flanders, this richly imagined historical novel follows a young painter's quest to unravel the identity of the mysterious woman at the centre of a famous portrait. Vanora Bennett writes with genuine erudition and a gift for period atmosphere.

Robert Harris's most recent thriller plunges a diplomat into the terrifying heart of a confrontation between Europe's nuclear powers in the final days of the Cold War. Precipice is a masterwork of political tension — meticulous, gripping, and utterly contemporary in its resonances.

The fourth in Dilly Court's heartwarming series following the Fairfax family through the Victorian East End returns at Christmas, weaving warmth, hardship, and the enduring bonds of family into a satisfying seasonal read.

An Irish wedding, two very different women, and the recipes passed down through generations form the heart of this warm and witty novel about love, loss, and the food that holds families together. Kate Kerrigan writes with real charm and emotional intelligence.

Amos Decker returns to his hometown in Mississippi to help an old army buddy — and walks into a conspiracy that has been decades in the making. Baldacci delivers his trademark combination of relentless plotting, moral seriousness, and a hero you cannot help but admire.

A copycat killer is reinventing the crimes of a long-dead serial murderer — and Detective Michael Bennett must stop him before the city descends into panic. Patterson's most muscular New York thriller, written with Andrew Gross, delivers relentless momentum from start to finish.

Fin Macleod returns to the Isle of Lewis to investigate the apparent suicide of an old friend — and uncovers a truth more terrible than anything he had imagined. Peter May's third and final Lewis trilogy novel is a masterpiece of atmosphere and emotional power.

A second copy of Peter May's devastating final Lewis novel — Fin Macleod's last case on the island that made him and broke him. Extraordinary atmospheric writing, an unforgettable setting, and a conclusion that earns every tear.

Detective John Rebus returns from the margins to investigate a cold case that links a dead body found in a church to the shadowy world of Edinburgh's criminal elite. Ian Rankin's sixteenth Rebus novel proves the old dog's teeth are still sharp.

When newly widowed Missy moves to a new neighbourhood, three women decide to befriend the lonely new arrival — and discover that saving someone else might be exactly what they each needed. Beth Morrey's debut is warm, funny, and quietly devastating. A perfect book club read.

Freya North's novel follows Pip, a young physiotherapist whose love life becomes beautifully complicated when she finds herself torn between two men she cares about deeply. North writes romance with genuine wit and emotional authenticity. Charming and wholly satisfying.

A junior doctor's reflections on life, death, and the extraordinary ordinary moments that fill a hospital ward. Aoife Abbey writes with rare clarity and compassion about what it means to care for people at their most vulnerable. Moving, honest, and essential reading.

When a woman's body is found in a forest in Hampshire, DI Jonah Sheens discovers that six young people who were camping nearby that night all have something to hide. Gytha Lodge's debut is a brilliantly constructed psychological thriller. A genuinely exciting new voice in crime fiction.

Nobel laureate Orhan Pamuk's exploration of memory, silence, and grief through the story of a man processing his father's death and his own complex relationship with Istanbul. Quiet, melancholy, and extraordinarily beautiful.

Lena and her twin sister Doris have been inseparable since childhood — until the war and a terrible choice drive them apart forever. Susan Lewis writes with emotional power about the impossible decisions that define a life. A deeply affecting wartime saga.

DS Marnie Rome investigates the death of a teenage girl whose mother has just been released from prison for trying to kill her — but the case is far more complicated than it first appears. Sarah Hilary's debut crime novel introduces one of the most psychologically complex detectives in recent British crime fiction.

When a basketball player from South Sudan gets the chance to fulfil his dreams at an American college, nothing could prepare him for what lies ahead. Grisham tells this based-on-real-events story with unusual warmth and a tenderness that shows a different side of his craft.

A woman's body is found on a Scottish hillside with her tongue removed — and the investigation draws Carol Jordan and Tony Hill into the most personal and disturbing case of their careers. Val McDermid at her most uncompromising and her most brilliant.

A practical, authoritative guide to residential property letting in the UK, covering everything from finding and screening tenants to managing repairs and navigating landlord law. An essential reference for anyone considering property investment.

column2: Sweet Revenge The Intimate Life Of Simon Cowell
column7: Tom Bower's unauthorised biography of Simon Cowell digs into the life of the most powerful man in the music industry — his ruthless business tactics, his complicated personal life, and the empire he built from scratch. Revealing, entertaining, and occasionally shocking.

Joyce Conway wakes up with no memory of the past thirty years of her life — but flashes of another woman's memories, a woman called Grace. Cecelia Ahern blends romantic warmth with something stranger and more moving in this inventive and deeply felt novel. One of her best.

Sarah Harrison's quietly brilliant novel follows three generations of a family whose choices ripple forward through time in ways they could never have anticipated. Elegantly constructed and emotionally precise. Deeply satisfying literary fiction.

John Maxwell's foundational leadership text articulates the universal laws that govern influence and authority — from the law of the lid to the law of legacy. Practical, inspiring, and backed by decades of leadership experience. Essential reading for anyone in a leadership role.

When Tom Kilbride agrees to house-sit for a neighbour, he thinks it will be a quiet few weeks — until someone starts trying to convince him that terrible things happened in that house. Linwood Barclay at his most cunningly plotted and deeply unsettling.

A woman arrives at a country house for Christmas and sets off a series of events that reveal everyone's secrets. Ali Smith's experimental novel defies easy summary but rewards the reader willing to trust its extraordinary intelligence. One of her most challenging and rewarding works.

Henry James's late masterpiece follows the American ambassador Lambert Strether to Paris, where he becomes enchanted by a world of sophistication and sensuality entirely unlike his own. A novel of extraordinary psychological subtlety and one of the great portraits of cultural collision.

The third and final volume of Philip Pullman's His Dark Materials trilogy brings Lyra and Will to the climactic battle for the fate of all worlds. Ambitious, emotionally devastating, and philosophically rich — a conclusion that earns its place alongside the greatest fantasy novels ever written.

Rugby union legend Richard Hill's candid account of his career with Saracens and England's World Cup-winning team, exploring the sport's transformation and his own evolution from player to coach. Essential reading for fans of English rugby.

Two people meet, fall in love, and lose each other — and the question is whether their story was a tragedy or a gift. Nicholas Sparks writes with his characteristic emotional precision about love, loss, and the choices that define us. Deeply moving and beautifully constructed.

Set in the same universe as Philip Pullman's His Dark Materials trilogy, La Belle Sauvage follows young Malcolm Polstead as he protects the infant Lyra from forces that will stop at nothing to possess her. Pullman's world-building is as magnificent as ever in this richly imagined prequel.

A legal thriller set in Biloxi, Mississippi, following two rival families whose feud reaches its bloody climax in a federal courtroom. Grisham blends his trademark courtroom drama with a rich evocation of Gulf Coast culture. One of his most atmospheric and satisfying novels.

Set in the early twentieth century, this family saga follows the Caldwell sisters through the upheavals of war, social change, and the shifting fortunes of a close-knit community. Rowena Summers writes with warmth and historical detail. Perfect for fans of sweeping generational sagas.

The third and final book in the Lewis Trilogy follows Fin Macleod to the remote island of the Flannan Isles, where three lighthouse keepers vanished without trace a century ago. Peter May delivers a mystery as haunting as the landscape itself. A breathtaking conclusion to an extraordinary series.

In 1940, a young woman vanishes from Birchwood Manor — and her unfinished portrait and her fate become obsessions for the people who pass through the house over the following decades. Kate Morton's intricate plotting and gorgeous prose are at their absolute finest. A masterpiece.

A British diplomat's wife dies in mysterious circumstances in Kenya, and her husband's attempt to discover the truth pulls back the curtain on the murderous compromises of the pharmaceutical industry. John le Carré's most morally urgent thriller. Devastating and brilliant.

When Mickey Haller's investigator is murdered, Mickey digs into the case himself — and uncovers a connection to one of Harry Bosch's old enemies. Connelly's crossover between his two greatest characters is brilliantly engineered. A treat for fans of either series.

Set in a tuberculosis sanatorium in 1940s London, two Jewish refugees — one from Germany, one from India — form an unlikely friendship under the shadow of war. Linda Grant writes with tremendous delicacy about displacement, identity, and survival. Beautifully crafted literary fiction.

column2: The Distracted Preacher And Other Tales
column7: Thomas Hardy's short fiction showcases the full range of his genius — from pastoral comedy to gothic tragedy — and provides an essential introduction to the moral complexity and landscape poetry of his longer novels.

The sinking of the Titanic becomes the backdrop for a riveting story of ambition, passion, and survival among the women who worked to dress the ship's first-class passengers. Kate Alcott writes vivid historical fiction with a compelling feminist edge.

The second Macro and Cato novel follows Rome's toughest soldiers into the mountains of Hispania for a brutal campaign against a guerrilla army. Simon Scarrow's military historical fiction is viscerally authentic and tremendously exciting. A worthy successor to Under the Eagle.

When a senator is found dead and a missing woman holds the key to understanding why, Amos Decker must navigate a conspiracy that reaches to the highest levels of Washington power. Baldacci delivers another hugely satisfying Decker thriller with genuine political resonance.

A woman returns to Andalusia to restore a garden and discovers the letters of a wartime love affair hidden in the ruins — and a secret that connects two eras. Rosanna Ley writes with warmth and sensory richness about memory, landscape, and the persistence of love.

When Nell O'Connor inherits a cottage on Dartmoor, she discovers a box of old photographs that lead her deep into a mystery spanning four generations. Kate Morton's second novel is a beautifully constructed puzzle-box of a book — immersive, emotionally rich, and gloriously satisfying.

John Galsworthy's Nobel Prize-winning saga follows three generations of the wealthy Forsyte family from the Victorian era to the 1920s, charting the rise and fall of a world defined by property and possession. One of the great English family chronicles. Rich, ironic, and endlessly absorbing.

column2: The Girl Who Kicked The Hornets' Nest
column7: Lisbeth Salander is hunted by two killers while facing trial for attempted murder in the electrifying third Millennium novel. Stieg Larsson brings his remarkable heroine's story to a furious, cathartic conclusion. One of the great thriller trilogies of the twenty-first century.

column2: The Girl Who Played With Fire
column7: Lisbeth Salander goes missing, a journalist is murdered, and Mikael Blomkvist discovers a conspiracy with roots in Sweden's darkest history. The second Millennium novel is even more propulsive than its predecessor, building the mythology of Lisbeth with extraordinary craft.

column2: The Girl With The Dragon Tattoo
column7: Journalist Mikael Blomkvist and hacker Lisbeth Salander investigate a forty-year-old disappearance and uncover a story of corruption and violence that has been hidden in plain sight. Stieg Larsson's debut novel launched a global phenomenon and introduced the most iconic heroine of modern crime fiction.

Catherine Cookson's powerful Victorian saga follows an illegitimate girl raised in the rural north of England who must fight every social convention to claim her identity and her right to be loved. Cookson writes her trademark themes of class, shame, and female resilience at their most potent.

A woman who sacrifices her own dreams to keep her family together and a man who fights for justice despite enormous personal cost — Danielle Steel tells a story of love under pressure with characteristic emotional force. Warm, compelling, and deeply felt.

A beautifully observed novel of life in a harbour town, tracing the intertwining lives of the people who live and work there across generations. Francesca Brill writes with quiet precision about community, belonging, and the tides of change.

A second edition of Francesca Brill's atmospheric harbour town saga — the same rich cast of characters navigating love, loss, and the rhythms of coastal life. For fans of literary fiction with deep roots in place and community.
Another copy of Francesca Brill's gently observed novel of harbour town life — ideal for readers who love character-driven literary fiction with a strong sense of place.

Edmund de Waal traces the journey of a collection of 264 Japanese netsuke through five generations of his family, from a Parisian salon to the Vienna of the Habsburgs to the Nazi occupation and beyond. A profound meditation on objects, memory, and what we carry with us across history.

Three maids in 1960s Jackson, Mississippi, risk everything to tell their stories to a white woman who wants to write a book about their lives. Kathryn Stockett's debut novel is a masterwork of empathy, voice, and moral courage. One of the most beloved novels of the twenty-first century.

A master assassin targets the most powerful men in Washington — and only Amos Decker, with his perfect memory, can identify the pattern before the next kill. Baldacci at his most kinetic, with a plot that races across 400 pages without pause.

A chance encounter on a ferry leads to an unexpected affair with consequences that ripple through two families in ways no one could have predicted. Sheila O'Flanagan writes with warmth and psychological insight about desire, decision, and the things we leave behind.

Set in a near-future world invaded by alien souls, Stephanie Meyer shifts from romance to science fiction with surprising confidence. Wanderer and Melanie Stryder share a body and must find a way to coexist in a world that has almost ceased to be human. Inventive and genuinely moving.

When Lena discovers that the house across the street was the scene of a terrible crime, she becomes obsessed with finding out what really happened. Lesley Pearse writes psychological suspense with her trademark emotional depth. Compulsive and deeply satisfying.

In 1924, a young woman joins the staff of Riverton Manor and sixty years later, on her deathbed, finally reveals the secret she has kept from the night a famous poet died. Kate Morton's debut novel is a beautifully constructed mystery of memory and desire. Captivating.

A young woman is invited into a wealthy household in early twentieth-century northern England and discovers that the invitation carries a terrible price. Catherine Cookson writes about the vulnerability of working-class women with characteristic power and emotional directness.

The last of Henry VIII's six wives is also the most underestimated — and Philippa Gregory gives Katherine Parr her full due in this richly imagined portrait of a woman who survived by her wits. Gregory at her most psychologically complex.

LAPD Detective Renée Ballard works the overnight shift at the Hollywood Division — and a single call leads her into a case that will consume her. Connelly's new heroine is one of his finest creations, and this first Ballard novel sets up her world with masterful economy.

Set in the turmoil of 1920s Russia, a young woman must use every resource she has to protect the extraordinary secrets she carries across a continent at war. Kate Furnivall writes sweeping historical fiction with tremendous emotional power.

column2: The Life And Loves Of A He Devil
column7: Graham Norton's memoir is as warm, funny, and candid as his chat show, tracing his journey from a shy Irish boy to one of Britain's most beloved television hosts. He writes about his sexuality, his demons, and his extraordinary career with disarming honesty.

column2: The Little Bed & Breakfast By The Sea
column7: A bed and breakfast by the sea, a new beginning, and an unexpected romance — Jennifer Joyce serves up pure feel-good fiction with charm, warmth, and a cast of characters you'll miss when the book ends. Perfect holiday reading.

Gervase Phinn's series of gentle memoirs about life as a schools inspector in the Yorkshire Dales reaches a charming new installment, full of eccentric teachers, wise children, and the particular warmth of a community built around education.

A quiz league with a small membership and an outsized sense of its own importance becomes the setting for a delightfully comic novel about competition, community, and the strange bonds that form around obscure hobbies.

A secret throne, an ancient conspiracy, and a race to uncover the truth before a shadowy organisation can bury it forever. Chris Kuzneski writes Da Vinci Code-style thriller with relentless momentum and a gift for unearthing genuinely fascinating history.

The moving story of a woman tormented by a secret that poisons her marriage and her life — and the man whose love may or may not be enough to save her. Catherine Cookson writes about female suffering and female endurance with characteristic power.

James Patterson brings his talent for propulsive, high-stakes plotting to the world of hospital obstetrics, where a series of maternal deaths turns out to be anything but natural. Co-written with James O. Born, this is Patterson in thriller-machine mode.

Amsterdam in 1936: a young woman arrives to work for a mysterious merchant and becomes obsessed with the miniature cabinet that mirrors her new household — with uncanny, prophetic precision. Jessie Burton's debut is a dazzling historical novel of extraordinary imagination and beauty.

column2: The One Memory Of Flora Banks
column7: Flora Banks has anterograde amnesia — she can't make new memories. But when she kisses a boy on a beach in France and writes it on her hand, something shifts. Emily Barr's taut, inventive thriller follows Flora across the Arctic Circle on a quest she won't remember taking.

When her marriage falls apart, Evie returns to her mother's cottage in rural Wales — and discovers a goose farm, an unexpected community, and a man she never planned to meet. Jo Thomas writes rural romance with tremendous warmth and comic timing.

Mark Honigsbaum traces the history of ten of the most devastating pandemics of the past century, from the Spanish flu to SARS, exploring the science, the politics, and the human cost of each outbreak. Essential and sobering reading.

A family gathering at a beloved summer house forces a reckoning with buried secrets that have shaped everyone's lives. Miranda Cowley Heller's debut is a beautifully written novel about desire, memory, and the impossible weight of family legacy.

When a seemingly simple debt collection job turns lethal, Danny Rawlings finds himself in the middle of a gang war he has no way out of. Simon Kernick writes propulsive, street-level crime fiction with real grit and energy.

Ken Follett's epic masterpiece follows the building of a cathedral in twelfth-century England across generations of builders, priests, and lords. One of the most beloved historical novels ever written — monumental in scope, deeply human in its emotions. An absolute must-read.

Lawrence Norfolk's extraordinary debut follows a complex chain of characters from the Vatican to Africa in pursuit of a wild rhinoceros destined for Pope Leo X. Brilliantly erudite, darkly comic, and unlike any other novel you will ever read.

The President of the United States goes missing — and only two people know where he is. James Patterson and Bill Clinton collaborate on a thriller with genuine insider authenticity, delivering the kind of White House drama that feels disturbingly plausible.

A young, idealistic lawyer takes on a tobacco company in a small-town wrongful death case — and discovers the terrifying lengths corporations will go to protect their profits. Grisham at his most righteously angry. A thriller that doubles as a devastating critique of corporate power.

Philippe Sands traces the wartime journey of SS officer Otto Wächter and his wife Charlotte through their own letters, diaries, and the testimony of their son. A masterwork of legal history, moral philosophy, and forensic biography that raises profound questions about guilt and responsibility.

Set in the Tudor court of Henry VIII, this novel follows the fierce rivalry between the White Rose of York and the Red Rose of Lancaster as the daughters of two dynasties fight for the future of England. Philippa Gregory at her most politically engaged.

When Elena visits Crete to learn Greek, she discovers the island's shattering wartime history through an elderly woman's story. Victoria Hislop's first novel launched her extraordinary career — richly atmospheric, deeply moving, and impossible to put down.

A young woman hiding from her past finds unexpected sanctuary in a Cornish community where the sea keeps secrets as well as the people do. Liz Fenwick writes evocative Cornish fiction with genuine emotional depth. Gorgeous sense of place.

In 1950s Moscow, Leo Demidov, an idealistic MGB officer, is forced to investigate a child murder the state insists is not happening. Tom Rob Smith's debut is a stunning thriller set in the paranoid heart of Stalin's Soviet Union. Tense, morally complex, and unforgettable.

Philippa Gregory turns her attention to the wives of Henry VIII with this richly imagined portrait of Katherine Howard, the fifth queen, tracing her short, brilliant, and doomed rise through the Tudor court. Vivid and psychologically astute.

When renowned crime writer Erica Falck returns to her childhood home in Sweden, she is drawn into a mystery surrounding the suicide of a woman she once knew. Camilla Läckberg's debut introduces one of Scandinavian crime fiction's most compelling heroines.

When bestselling author Lissy Brown inherits a share of a Shropshire holiday village from a mysterious benefactor, she must work alongside its frustratingly handsome co-owner. Cathy Bramley blends laugh-out-loud humour with a genuinely touching love story.

column2: The Tales Of Beedle The Bard
column7: J.K. Rowling's companion volume to the Harry Potter series presents the wizarding fairy tales that Harry, Ron, and Hermione read as children. Charming, inventive, and rich with the mythology of Rowling's world, this is a delight for fans of all ages.

A young woman born into poverty must fight for her right to love and be loved in the face of a world that sees her as property. Catherine Cookson's commanding voice and deep emotional intelligence make this one of her most powerful novels.

Set in a Yorkshire toffee factory in the early twentieth century, this warm-hearted saga follows the women workers whose lives are bound together by friendship, hardship, and the bitter-sweet business of making something beautiful. Glenda Young at her most readable.

column2: The Unlikely Pilgrimage Of Harold Fry
column7: Harold Fry sets off to post a letter and ends up walking the length of England to save a dying friend. Rachel Joyce's debut novel is a quiet miracle — a profound meditation on regret, redemption, and the possibility of being reborn at any age. One of the great novels of the decade.

Anthony Trollope's great novel of Victorian commerce follows the rise and fall of Augustus Melmotte, a financier of uncertain origins whose apparent wealth dazzles London society. A devastatingly accurate portrait of the corrupting power of money. More relevant today than ever.

When a brilliant judge begins receiving death threats after investigating a corrupt court system in Florida, she turns to Jake Brigance for help. Grisham delivers his trademark blend of legal procedure, Southern atmosphere, and moral complexity in an unputdownable thriller.

Elizabeth of York lives at the dangerous heart of the Wars of the Roses, forced to choose between the men competing for the throne — and for her. Philippa Gregory's first Cousins' War novel is propulsive, richly imagined, and driven by a heroine of formidable intelligence.

Wilkie Collins's Victorian sensation novel follows the investigations of Walter Hartright as he attempts to unravel a conspiracy of mistaken identity that has destroyed one woman's life. One of the first — and still one of the greatest — mystery novels ever written.

Frank McCourt's sequel to Angela's Ashes follows young Frankie to New York in 1949, where the promised land turns out to be a good deal more complicated than he had imagined. McCourt's voice is as alive and funny as ever in this deeply human sequel.

Virginia Woolf's experimental masterpiece follows the Ramsay family across two visits to their holiday home in the Hebrides, meditating on time, loss, and the nature of art itself. To the Lighthouse is one of the great novels of modernism — hypnotic, devastating, and transcendently beautiful.

Henry Fielding's sprawling comic epic follows the adventures of the foundling Tom Jones across eighteenth-century England, encountering rogues, beauties, and the full absurd comedy of human nature along the way. A foundational novel of English literature and still enormously entertaining.

A warm and funny memoir from beloved TV gardener Alan Titchmarsh about the trials, disasters, and unexpected joys of learning to garden. Written with his characteristic warmth and self-deprecating humour, it is a delight for gardeners and non-gardeners alike.

A man helping an agoraphobic woman monitor the street outside her window believes he has witnessed a murder — but no one will believe him. Linwood Barclay's standalone thriller is a tightly wound puzzle that keeps you doubting everything until the final revelation.

Harriet Beecher Stowe's 1852 abolitionist novel brought the horror of American slavery into the homes of millions of readers and helped change the course of history. Uncle Tom's Cabin is a landmark of world literature — deeply flawed in places by its era, yet undeniably important.

Macro and Cato join the Second Legion as Rome prepares to invade Britain in the first novel in Simon Scarrow's brilliant Eagle series. Viscerally authentic military historical fiction that sets up one of the genre's great partnerships. Compelling from its opening pages.

Dr Richard Shepherd has attended thousands of crime and disaster scenes as one of Britain's leading forensic pathologists. His memoir is a forensic examination of death itself — what it tells us about the living and the cases that haunted him most. Extraordinarily compelling.

Thackeray's sprawling masterpiece follows the scheming Becky Sharp as she claws her way up through the social hierarchies of Regency England with razor-sharp intelligence and zero moral restraint. One of the great satirical novels of the nineteenth century. Brilliant and biting.

Set on the Greek island of Kefalonia, a British woman discovers the wartime journals of a young Greek woman whose story becomes an obsession. Patricia Wilson writes evocative Mediterranean historical fiction with real emotional power. Beautifully atmospheric.

Charlotte Brontë's second novel follows Lucy Snowe, an Englishwoman adrift in the fictional Belgian city of Villette, navigating love and loneliness with fierce intelligence. Darker and more psychologically complex than Jane Eyre, it is one of the great novels of the Victorian era.

Will Self's experimental travelogue blurs autobiography, fiction, and cultural criticism as he makes his way to Hollywood through a series of surreal encounters. Demanding, brilliant, and unlike anything else — for readers who like their literary fiction genuinely challenging.

David Nott is a surgeon who has volunteered in some of the world's most dangerous conflict zones, from Bosnia to Syria. His memoir is as gripping as any thriller and as morally serious as a reckoning with war itself can be. One of the most important memoirs of our time.

Set during the Second World War, this emotionally rich saga follows two families separated by conflict and reunited by unexpected bonds. Margaret Dickinson writes her trademark combination of social history, family drama, and female resilience with great skill.

DCI Banks returns to the Yorkshire Dales to investigate a murder that connects a detective's past to a present-day conspiracy. Peter Robinson writes atmospheric British crime fiction at a high level, and this Banks novel is one of his most satisfying.

What If? is Randall Munroe's hilarious and scientifically rigorous exploration of hypothetical questions — from what would happen if you fired a gun in space to how high you could throw a baseball. Brilliant, funny, and unexpectedly educational.

A moving saga of a street pedlar's life in the East End of London, following his family through poverty, hardship, and the small moments of grace that sustain people through the worst. Harry Bowling writes working-class history with genuine warmth and authenticity.

Rosie Dunne and Alex Stewart have been best friends since childhood, exchanging hundreds of emails and letters across an ocean — but somehow the right words always come too late. Cecelia Ahern's first novel is a heartbreaking and heartwarming story of love nearly missed. Irresistible.

Hilary Mantel's Booker Prize-winning masterpiece follows Thomas Cromwell from his brutal childhood to the corridors of Henry VIII's court, where he must outmanoeuvre everyone — including himself. One of the greatest historical novels ever written. Transformative.

Lena never expected to see her husband again after he faked his own death — but when he reappears, the truth he carries is more dangerous than anything she could have imagined. Lesley Pearse writes psychological suspense with real emotional power.

A thriller writer discovers a body in her house — and the evidence points directly at her. James Patterson and Howard Roughan construct a plot of dizzying plausibility that keeps you flipping pages until the shocking conclusion. A standout Patterson standalone.
